checks.a_analysis: I have compared the text in the JSON output with the source document. Every text element in the JSON, including headings, legend labels, and sources, is derived directly from the source PDF. There are no fabricated paragraphs, sentences, or sections. The metadata title is an expected AI-generated addition for accessibility.
The source document contains no phone numbers, emails, or URLs. The JSON output also contains no contact information. Thus, no fabrication has occurred.

The source document consists of a single page containing a title, date, map, legend, and sources. All of these components are present in the JSON output. The title and date are captured as headings, the legend as a list, the source info as a paragraph, and the map and logo as image elements.
The source document does not contain any tables. The parcel update status categories are presented as a legend on the map, which has been correctly converted into lists in the JSON output.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All major text sections (title, date, legend text, and sources) have been captured. While the text labels for the scale bar ('0 25 50 100 Miles') are not present as discrete text elements in the JSON, they are part of the graphical scale bar which is included within the map image element (reading_order 6). The omission of these few words as text does not constitute a significant text omission (>25%) of the document's content.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON output matches the text in the source document exactly in terms of meaning. The labels in the legend, the date, the main title, and the sources are all perfectly transcribed without any alteration of dates, names, or status categories.

checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the output JSON against the source PDF. Each unique text segment from the source, including the main titles, the legend items, and the sources section, appears only once in the output. There are no instances of duplicated paragraphs, lists, or headings.

The output follows a logical heading hierarchy. The main title 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' is correctly assigned H1 (reading_order 1), and the date 'January 8, 2025' follows as an H2 (reading_order 2). There are no skipped levels or incorrect nesting.
The source document does not contain any tables. The data presented in the legend is appropriately structured as lists in the output.

The reading order in the output follows a logical progression that respects the source document's layout. It begins with the titles, proceeds through the legend information (lists at reading_order 3 and 4), the source citations (reading_order 5), and finally the visual elements (images at reading_order 6 and 7). While the legend text is placed before the map image, this is a standard accessibility adjustment to provide context before visual data.

The legend section of the map, which is visually a list, has been correctly identified and converted into unordered list structures. The first list (reading_order 3) captures the legend header and sub-header, while the second list (reading_order 4) captures the specific status categories (e.g., 'Processing for 2025 Q1', '2024 Q4'). This accurately preserves the meaning and structure of the source.
The source document contains no URLs or hyperlinks. Accordingly, no links were injected or omitted in the output JSON.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF is a map-based infographic where information is organized spatially across a map of North Carolina with a legend and scale bar. The HTML output completely lacks the map element and its spatial organization. Instead, it presents the legend items as a simple vertical bulleted list. This is a significant departure from the source's visual organization, changing it from a graphical information display to a text list. The visual flow no longer matches the source's intent.
checks.b_evidence_source: A geographical map of North Carolina counties with color-coded fills and labels, organized spatially with a legend in the bottom left and logos in the corners.
checks.b_evidence_output: A linear vertical layout containing a bulleted list of text representing the legend items. No map or spatial organization is present.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF does not contain any tables. The data is presented via a map and a legend list. Therefore, there are no tables to be misrendered.

The most critical visual elements of the source document are missing in the HTML output. The central map of North Carolina, the NCGICC logo, the compass rose, and the scale bar are all absent. While the HTML includes text placeholders/alt-text for the map and the logo ('A map of North Carolina counties...' and 'NCGICC logo'), the actual visual assets are not rendered in the output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The map of North Carolina (center), the compass rose (top left), the scale bar (bottom center), and the NCGICC logo (bottom right).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows text descriptions where images should be, but no graphical images are rendered.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the HTML output and do not find any instances of body content (paragraphs, list items, or tables) being duplicated. The text from the source legend and the source description appears only once in the main body of the HTML.
